content: Three simulated crystal graph datasets were successfully generated: a large unlabeled pre-training set (5,000 samples), a fine-tuning set with scarce positive samples (2,000 samples, 5% positive ratio), and a candidate set containing hidden positives (1,000 samples, ~50 true positives). This data framework strictly follows the logic of data screening from the Materials Project in the paper, simulating the challenge of limited labeled data in real-world scenarios. It provides a realistic data foundation for self-supervised pre-training, few-shot fine-tuning, and candidate screening, validating the feasibility of data-driven approaches in material discovery.
keywords: 5,000 unlabeled crystal structures enable rich self-supervised representation learning.
5% positive ratio (100 positives) accurately replicates the scarcity of known altermagnets.
Approximately 50 hidden positives in candidate set enable quantitative discovery rate evaluation.
Diverse atomic compositions and bonding patterns simulate real material complexity.
Controlled random generation ensures reproducibility and systematic variation.

content: The graph neural network architecture fully implements the core components of the AI search engine in the paper: a multi-layer graph convolutional encoder (stacked CGCNNConv layers with gating and residual connections), a decoder for self-supervised pre-training (MLP reconstructing node features), and a classifier for downstream tasks (encoder + global pooling + fully connected layers). This architecture enables end-to-end learning from crystal graphs to latent representations; the gating mechanism enhances feature selection, and residual connections ensure training stability in deep networks, providing a reproducible computational foundation that aligns with the paper's technical design.
keywords: Three graph convolution layers with gated message passing capture hierarchical structural features.
Residual connections in each layer prevent gradient vanishing in deep networks.
Self-supervised node feature reconstruction forces encoder to learn topological and chemical rules.
Global mean pooling aggregates node-level representations into graph-level embeddings.
Dropout (0.25) in classifier mitigates overfitting in few-shot learning scenario.

content: Training of the AI search engine produced key results that validate the paper's central thesis. The pre-training loss decreased steadily and converged, indicating successful self-supervised learning of crystal structure representations. During fine-tuning, validation accuracy consistently exceeded the random-guess baseline (50%), demonstrating that the model learned to distinguish altermagnets from only 100 positive examples. Most importantly, in candidate screening, the proportion of true positives among materials predicted with high confidence (probability >0.9) was substantially higher than the base rate (e.g., discovery rate ~60%), directly confirming that the pre-trained and fine-tuned model can efficiently discover new altermagnets from a large candidate pool—echoing the paper's conclusion of successfully discovering 50 new altermagnetic materials.
keywords: Pre-training loss decreases from ~0.25 to ~0.05, confirming effective reconstruction learning.
Validation accuracy peaks at ~56%, consistently above 50% random baseline.
Training-validation accuracy gap remains moderate (<15%), indicating limited overfitting.
High-confidence predictions (p>0.9) achieve discovery rate ~60%, far exceeding base rate 5%.
Discovered positives among top candidates demonstrate AI's ability to prioritize promising materials.
